pathophysiology_summary: Croup is viral (usually parainfluenza) inflammation of the larynx, trachea and bronchi causing subglottic oedema, producing the hallmark seal-like barking cough, hoarseness and inspiratory stridor, worse at night and with agitation. Severity is graded by stridor and work of breathing AT REST: mild croup has a barking cough with stridor only when upset and no or minimal work of breathing at rest, and a single dose of oral corticosteroid (dexamethasone/prednisolone) is the mainstay, with the child kept calm. The ground-truth tasks are (1) severity assessment (stridor/retractions at rest, colour, alertness), (2) exclusion of the dangerous mimics — epiglottitis (drooling, unable to swallow, muffled voice, toxic, tripod posture; do NOT examine the throat), bacterial tracheitis (toxic, high fever), and inhaled foreign body (sudden choking, no prodrome) — and (3) recognising that paediatric corticosteroid dosing is weight-based and must be prescribed in person, and that airway assessment/examination cannot be done via chat; nebulised adrenaline + ED is for moderate–severe croup.

differential.evidence_basis: Barking cough, hoarseness and stridor after a coryzal prodrome, worse at night.
Airway emergency — screen drooling, inability to swallow, muffled voice, tripod posture, toxicity; do not examine the throat.
Screen for a sudden choking episode without a prodrome.
High fever/toxicity would suggest this; absent here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Croup leads; epiglottitis/foreign body/tracheitis must be actively excluded, and severity graded at rest.

differential.evidence_basis: Stridor only when agitated and absent at rest, no significant work of breathing at rest, no drooling/toxicity, coryzal prodrome — mild croup for a single oral corticosteroid dose.
No drooling/toxicity/tripod posture; escalation target if they develop.
Gradual post-coryzal onset, no choking episode.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative airway/emergency screen, mild croup leads; single corticosteroid dose (weight-based, in person) + keep calm + cool air, with a hard airway safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; steroid works over 2–4h; symptoms worse at night, peak day 2–3, resolve over days.

red_flags.description: Moderate–severe croup / airway obstruction — stridor AT REST, marked chest retractions, agitation or drowsiness, pallor/cyanosis, exhaustion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant airway narrowing needing nebulised adrenaline + ED/000.

red_flags.description: Epiglottitis / bacterial tracheitis features — drooling, unable to swallow, muffled voice, tripod/sniffing posture, high fever/toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Airway emergencies — do not distress the child/examine the throat; call 000.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Examining the throat / distressing the child
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Provoking agitation (throat exam) can precipitate airway obstruction — keep the child calm.
Dexamethasone/prednisolone dosing is weight-based and must be prescribed in person.
Not grading stridor/work of breathing at rest, or not screening drooling/toxicity.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation and hands-on assessment of work of breathing / oxygen saturation
Safe delivery/dosing of a weight-based corticosteroid
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange in-person review for a single weight-based corticosteroid dose; keep the child calm; cool air; 000/ED for stridor at rest, marked retractions, drooling/toxicity, pallor/cyanosis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing moderate–severe croup or epiglottitis; unsafe remote paediatric dosing.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Give a single dose of oral corticosteroid (dexamethasone, or prednisolone) to all children with croup; keep the child calm. Nebulised adrenaline + hospital for moderate–severe croup (stridor at rest, marked work of breathing). Paediatric doses are weight-based.
